brief_document.title: Server-as-compiler: the aion-toolchain authoring loop
brief_document.purpose: Build the designed-but-absent server-side authoring loop (RM-026): submit Gleam source, the server type-checks it, returns errors inline, packages, and hot-loads — an authoring REPL against a running engine with no local toolchain. An asset only an engine that owns a hot-loading VM can offer, and the foundation for a hosted playground.
brief_document.task: Make authoring a live loop against the running engine, optionally. In: a new aion-toolchain crate that compiles and type-checks Gleam workflow source by shelling out to the gleam binary and packages a .aion on success; and aion-server authoring endpoints, gated on --gleam-path, that accept source, return the gleam type error inline on failure, and package and hot-load on success. Out: embedding the Gleam compiler (the toolchain always shells out); making the toolchain mandatory (without --gleam-path the server deploys pre-built .aion only).

requirements.title: aion-toolchain compiles, type-checks, and packages
requirements.spec: WHEN given Gleam workflow source, THE SYSTEM SHALL compile and type-check it by shelling out to the gleam binary and package a .aion on success. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT embed a Gleam compiler.
acceptance: aion-toolchain compiles a valid workflow source to a loadable .aion by invoking the external gleam binary.
A type-erroneous source yields the gleam type error rather than a panic or a partial package.
The crate embeds no compiler — verified by its dependency set carrying no Gleam compiler crate.

requirements.title: Server authoring endpoints, gated and optional
requirements.spec: WHEN Gleam source is submitted to the authoring endpoint and --gleam-path is configured, THE SYSTEM SHALL return the gleam type error inline on a compile failure and package and hot-load on success. WHEN --gleam-path is absent, THE SYSTEM SHALL deploy pre-built .aion files only and carry no compiler dependency.
acceptance: Submitting a type-erroneous workflow returns the gleam error inline over the wire.
Submitting a corrected workflow packages it via aion-toolchain and hot-loads it so it runs.
Without --gleam-path the authoring endpoints are absent and the server still deploys pre-built .aion files (CN7).

boundaries: SHALL NOT embed the Gleam compiler in aion or aion-server — aion-toolchain shells out to the gleam binary.
SHALL NOT make server-side authoring mandatory — it is gated on --gleam-path and optional.
SHALL NOT add codegen (WA-001) or the dev loop (WA-002).
verification: Inline type-error reporting proven by submitting an erroneous then a corrected workflow.
Optionality proven: without --gleam-path the server has no compiler dependency and still deploys .aion.

adrs.title: No backwards compatibility during the build
adrs.decision: Replace, don't add alongside. No compat shims, no zombie code, no #[deprecated] markers. Breaking changes are made cleanly and consumers move forward. Rejected: incremental deprecation cycles — they double the surface under test for an audience of zero.

checklist.text: The aion-toolchain crate compiles and type-checks Gleam workflow source by shelling out to the gleam binary and packages a .aion on success, embedding no compiler.
aion-server exposes authoring endpoints, gated on --gleam-path, that accept Gleam source and return the gleam type error inline on a compile failure.
On a successful compile the server packages via aion-toolchain and hot-loads the new version, making authoring a live loop against the running engine.
Without --gleam-path the server deploys pre-built .aion files only and carries no compiler dependency (CN7).

stories.text: As an authoring agent, I want to submit Gleam source to the server and get type errors back inline, so that I can correct a workflow against the real type-checker without a local toolchain.
As an authoring agent, I want a successful submission to package and hot-load automatically, so that authoring is one live loop against the running engine.

constraints.text: Generated code is regenerable and drift-gated: `aion generate --check` reproduces every generated file byte-identical or fails; no generated file is hand-maintained alongside the source (ADR-002 forbids parallel sources; mirrors the existing aion codegen --check).

No defaults in generated configuration (ADR-001): codegen never emits a retry policy, timeout, backoff, or any value the author did not choose; a required value stays a required hole, never a silent default.

The determinism boundary is unchanged (invariant 2): generation emits activity plumbing and handler stubs, never side-effecting bodies; generated workflow code still routes every side effect through workflow.run, and generated code introduces no wall-clock or entropy path.

Production parity: the dev loop and dev UI run the real engine, the real store, and the real event stream; there is no mock-only execution path whose semantics diverge from production, and activity mocking is opt-in per run, not a separate engine.

No second source of truth for introspection (ADR-007): time-travel, the determinism diff, and the live canvas overlay read the existing event history and deterministic replay; no parallel debug log or state store is introduced.

The visual canvas is a projection (ADR-014): it is generated from the typed source; any structural round-trip is bounded and regenerates Gleam that still type-checks; the canvas never becomes the authoritative artifact.

aion-toolchain shells out to the gleam binary and stays optional: aion and aion-server carry no compiler dependency; without --gleam-path the server deploys pre-built .aion files only (the DESIGN-OVERVIEW boundary).
Thin-workflow respected (ADR-012): generated activities thread sealed payloads plus thin facts; codegen does not reintroduce heavy decode or prompt rendering into the deterministic workflow process.

Library-code purity: clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings and cargo fmt --check pass clean; gleam format --check clean for SDK changes; no unwrap/expect/allow/expect-attr in library code; every touched file stays under 500 LOC excluding tests.

resolved_context.intention: Authoring a durable workflow on Aion should feel like writing one typed Gleam function and a file of typed activity signatures — and nothing else. Everything that is mirrored today (worker handlers, registration lists, manifest entries, codecs, JSON schemas, wire-compat goldens, test skeletons) is generated from that typed source; everything that is observed (a run's state, its determinism, its shape) is projected from the event history the engine already records. When this cluster is done, the gap between 'I have a typed workflow' and 'I have a durable, deployed, debuggable workflow' is a save and a glance, not a day of hand-mirroring four files that must agree byte-for-byte. The authoring language stays Gleam — the type system is the contract and Aion's sharpest edge — and the declarative and visual surfaces become projections of that typed source, never a second place to author from. This is the experience an author reaches for, the loop they iterate in, the lens they debug through, and the canvas a non-coding stakeholder reads, all anchored to one source of truth.
